cards.title: Functional Medicine Lab Panel — Overview
cards._legacyHtml:
Beyond standard blood tests
Functional medicine labs uncover root causes that standard panels miss — subclinical deficiencies, metabolic dysfunction, toxic burden, and microbiome disruption — often years before disease manifests.
CategoryKey TestsWhat they reveal
MethylationHomocysteine, RBC folate, holotranscobalamin, MTHFR geneB-vitamin status, cardiovascular risk, neurotransmitter capacity
Inflammationhs-CRP, fibrinogen, IL-6, ferritin, ESRChronic low-grade inflammation, cardiovascular risk stratification
Metabolic healthFasting insulin, HOMA-IR, HbA1c, fasting glucose, triglycerides:HDL ratioInsulin resistance (often years before T2DM diagnosis)
Thyroid (full)TSH, free T3, free T4, reverse T3, anti-TPO, anti-TgAbConversion issues, subclinical Hashimoto's, functional hypo
Adrenal/cortisol4-point salivary cortisol, DHEAS, cortisol awakening responseHPA axis pattern, burnout, adrenal dysfunction
Nutrients25-OH-D3, RBC magnesium, RBC zinc, plasma copper, ferritin, omega-3 indexFunctional deficiencies (serum values unreliable)
GutStool microbiome (PCR-based), calprotectin, zonulin, secretory IgA, elastaseDysbiosis, leaky gut, IBD, EPI
HormonesDUTCH test (urine) — oestrogen metabolites, androgens, cortisol metabolitesOestrogen dominance, androgen excess, cortisol patterns
Organic acids (OAT)Urine organic acids — 70+ metabolitesMitochondrial function, neurotransmitters, gut bacteria, nutrient status
ToxicityHeavy metals (RBC or urine), mycotoxins (urine), glyphosateEnvironmental toxic burden — often a missed root cause
🟡 Where to start
For most new patients, start with: (1) Full thyroid panel, (2) Fasting insulin + HOMA-IR, (3) hs-CRP, (4) Homocysteine, (5) 25-OH-D3, (6) Ferritin, (7) RBC magnesium. This 7-marker panel costs <₹3000 and reveals the most common functional imbalances in Indian naturopathic practice. Expand to DUTCH or OAT based on findings.

cards.title: HOMA-IR — Insulin Resistance Assessment
cards._legacyHtml:
HOMA-IR formula
Fasting insulin × fasting glucose / 22.5
Optimal
<1.0
Insulin resistant
>2.0
Significant IR
>3.0
HOMA-IRInterpretationNaturopathic Action
<1.0Optimal insulin sensitivityMaintain — Mediterranean diet, exercise
1.0–2.0Borderline — early insulin resistanceLow-GI diet, TRE (16:8), HIIT exercise, berberine 500mg TID
2.0–3.5Insulin resistant — pre-diabetic riskAbove + inositol 4g/day, cinnamon, chromium, ACV before meals, reduce carbs
>3.5Significant IR — T2DM risk, PCOS, NAFLDMedical co-management + intensive lifestyle: ketogenic trial, berberine, metformin discussion
🟡 Fasting insulin — most overlooked test
Fasting glucose and HbA1c are normal until insulin resistance is advanced. Fasting insulin becomes elevated 5–15 years before HbA1c rises — it is the earliest marker of metabolic dysfunction. Normal lab range for fasting insulin: 2–25 mIU/L. But OPTIMAL is <8 mIU/L. Insulin >10 fasting = insulin resistance even with normal glucose. Request it for every patient with: central obesity, fatigue, sugar cravings, PCOS, NAFLD, skin tags, acanthosis nigricans.

cards.title: Organic Acids Test (OAT) — Interpretation Guide
cards._legacyHtml:
What it is
Urine organic acids test analyses 70+ metabolic byproducts to assess mitochondrial function, neurotransmitter metabolism, gut bacteria/yeast overgrowth, and nutritional status — in a single urine sample.
OAT CategoryKey MarkersIf ElevatedIf Low
Yeast/fungalArabinose ★, citramalicCandida overgrowth, dysbiosisN/A
Bacterial (clostridia)HPHPA, 4-cresolClostridium overgrowth → dopamine disruption, autism spectrumN/A
MitochondrialCitric acid cycle intermediates (succinate, fumarate, malate)Blockage in TCA cycleMitochondrial under-function
NeurotransmitterHVA/VMA (dopamine), 5-HIAA (serotonin), quinolinateQuinolinate ↑ = neuroinflammation, IDO activation↓ Serotonin/dopamine metabolites
B-vitaminsMethylmalonic acid (B12), xanthurenic (B6), kynurenic (B6)Functional deficiency of respective vitaminN/A
Oxidative stress8-hydroxydeoxyguanosine, pyroglutamateOxidative DNA damage, glutathione depletionN/A
🟡 Arabinose — the Candida marker
Arabinose is produced by Candida/yeast fermentation in the gut and excreted in urine. Elevated arabinose = gut yeast overgrowth. Symptoms: sugar cravings, brain fog, fatigue, bloating, recurrent thrush, nail fungal infections. Treatment: low-sugar diet, antifungal herbs (oregano oil, caprylic acid, berberine), restore Lactobacillus with probiotics. Most cost-effective OAT lab in India: Great Plains Laboratory (US, send sample) or Vibrant Wellness.

cards.title: Advanced Lipid Testing — Beyond LDL
cards._legacyHtml:
Standard lipid panel misses 50% of cardiovascular risk
Half of MI patients have "normal" LDL. Advanced lipid testing identifies particle size, number, and oxidation — the true drivers of atherosclerosis.
MarkerOptimalHigh RiskClinical Significance
ApoB (apolipoprotein B)<90 mg/dL (<70 if high risk)>130 mg/dLBest single predictor of CVD — counts ALL atherogenic particles (LDL + VLDL + IDL)
Lp(a) — lipoprotein(a)<30 mg/dL (<75 nmol/L)>50 mg/dLGenetically determined; independent CVD risk; niacin ↓ Lp(a); statins do NOT lower it
sdLDL (small dense LDL)Low pattern APattern B (small, dense)3× more atherogenic than large LDL; driven by insulin resistance + high carb diet
Triglycerides:HDL ratio<1.5 (mmol/L) or <1.0>3.0Best surrogate for insulin resistance + small dense LDL. High TG:HDL = metabolic syndrome
Oxidised LDL (oxLDL)LowElevatedThe truly atherogenic form of LDL — requires antioxidants to reduce. Reduced by: polyphenols, olive oil, curcumin, vitamin E
hs-CRP<0.5 mg/L (ideal)>3.0 mg/LInflammatory CVD risk; reduced by omega-3, statins (JUPITER), Mediterranean diet
🟡 ApoB vs LDL-C
ApoB is superior to LDL-C for CVD risk assessment. Each atherogenic lipoprotein particle carries exactly one ApoB molecule — so ApoB directly counts particles. You can have "normal" LDL-C with high ApoB (many small dense particles) = high risk. Or high LDL-C with low ApoB (few large fluffy particles) = lower risk. European Atherosclerosis Society now recommends ApoB as the primary treatment target. Request it on every cardiac risk patient.

cards.title: Comprehensive Thyroid Panel — Interpretation
cards._legacyHtml:
Beyond TSH — The Full Thyroid Picture
Standard care measures TSH only. Full panel reveals: conversion problems, autoimmunity, nutrient deficiencies, and rT3 excess — all missed by TSH alone.
MarkerConventional RangeFunctional OptimalSignificance
TSH0.4–4.5 mIU/L1.0–2.5 mIU/LHigh = hypothyroid. Low = hyperthyroid. Many patients symptomatic in upper "normal" range.
Free T410–20 pmol/L14–19 pmol/LT4 production — if low with normal TSH = secondary hypothyroidism
Free T3 ★3.5–6.5 pmol/L5.0–6.5 pmol/LActive hormone. Low with normal TSH/T4 = conversion problem (stress, selenium def, liver)
Reverse T3 (rT3)<24 ng/dL<15 ng/dLHigh = blocks T3 receptors. Caused by: chronic stress, low calorie diet, illness, selenium def
TPO antibodies<35 IU/mL<5 IU/mLElevated = Hashimoto's thyroiditis (autoimmune)
Thyroglobulin Ab<115 IU/mL<10 IU/mLElevated = Hashimoto's or Graves' (less specific)
TSH receptor Ab (TRAb)<1.75 IU/LAbsentElevated = Graves' disease (stimulating antibody)
🟡 Normal TSH But Hypothyroid Symptoms?
Pattern: Normal TSH + normal fT4 + LOW fT3 + HIGH rT3 = peripheral conversion failure. Patient feels hypothyroid (fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, brain fog) but told labs are normal. Naturopathic approach: selenium 200mcg/day, stress reduction, adequate calories, treat gut and liver. This pattern is increasingly recognised clinically.

cards.title: Stool Analysis, DUTCH Test & Heavy Metal Testing
cards._legacyHtml:
COMPREHENSIVE STOOL ANALYSIS (GI-MAP)
MarkerElevated SuggestsLow Suggests
Calprotectin>50 = IBD, infection. >200 = active IBD flareFunctional disorder (IBS) rather than IBD
Secretory IgA (sIgA)Acute GI stress, infectionImmune dysfunction, high cortisol (stress depletes sIgA)
Pancreatic elastase-1N/A<200 µg/g = exocrine pancreatic insufficiency — poor fat digestion
Beta-glucuronidaseOestrogen dominance, dysbiosis (↑ oestrogen reabsorption)Normal
H. pylori antigenActive H. pylori infection — treatNormal or post-eradication
Occult bloodAny positive → colonoscopy (colorectal cancer until proven otherwise)Normal
DUTCH TEST (Dried Urine Comprehensive Hormones)
MarkerWhat It RevealsClinical Value
Free cortisol (4-point) ★Diurnal cortisol curve — AM peak, midday, evening, nightHPA dysfunction pattern. Cannot get from single serum cortisol.
2-OHE1 vs 4-OHE1 vs 16-OHE1 ★Oestrogen metabolite pathways2-OH = protective. 4-OH = genotoxic. 16-OH = proliferative. DIM shifts toward 2-OH.
Melatonin (DLMO)Circadian melatonin timingLow = blue light, stress. Guides melatonin supplement timing.
HEAVY METAL TESTING
MetalBest TestKey Indication
LeadBlood lead (recent exposure)Old painted buildings, industrial areas, imported spices, children
MercuryHair mercury (dietary/fish exposure) · Blood (recent)High fish consumption, dental amalgam, occupational
ArsenicSpeciated urine arsenic ★ (not total — dietary arsenobetaine from shellfish is harmless)Well water, rice-heavy diet, occupational
🟡 IgG Food Sensitivity Testing — The Evidence Problem
IgG4 food sensitivity tests are widely marketed but IgG4 elevation indicates EXPOSURE, not sensitivity — high IgG4 means you eat the food frequently, not that you react to it. Evidence bodies (BSACI, EAACI) do not recommend IgG food testing. Gold standard for food intolerance: 3–6 week elimination diet + structured rechallenge + symptom diary. For true allergy: refer allergist for IgE RAST or skin prick testing. For coeliac: TTG-IgA + total IgA — never start GFD before testing.
